title: OpenTitan I2C fall-time (t_f) double-counting
tagline: I2C counts the fall time twice — every clock runs too slow.
hook_30s: The I2C controller holds SCL low for t_low plus the bus fall time — counted once. This one adds the fall time twice. Every clock cycle SCL is held low one fall-time too long, so the bus runs slower than configured and blows the timing budget. ChipGPT catches it with a directed test measuring the SCL low period.

proof.note: Protocol timing — the natural proof is a directed test that measures the computed SCL low period against the spec model (this is how the real OpenTitan fix shipped: with a DIF test). Formal could also check the arithmetic.

steps.agent: Task: check the I2C SCL low-period computation against the spec timing model.
Reading the I2C timing rule: SCL low period = t_low + t_f, with the fall time counted exactly once.
Parsing i2c_timing.sv. Inspecting the scl_low_o expression.

BUG. scl_low_o = t_low_i + t_f_i + t_f_i — the fall time t_f is added TWICE. The spec says once (t_low + t_f). SCL is held low one extra t_f every cycle, so the bus runs slower than configured and the timing budget is violated. CWE-1298.
Directed test: drive t_low and t_f, compare scl_low_o against the spec model t_low + t_f.

artifact.name: t_i2c_scl_low_period
artifact.result_on_buggy: FAIL — scl_low_o = 124 (= 100 + 12 + 12), one t_f too long

steps.agent: Pinning the spec-correct relation as an assertion (also formally checkable).

artifact.explanation: SCL low must equal t_low plus exactly one fall time.
artifact.result: Violated by current RTL (t_f counted twice); directed sim is the primary proof here.

artifact.title: I2C fall-time (t_f) double-counting
artifact.root_cause: scl_low = t_low + t_f + t_f; the fall time is added twice instead of once.
artifact.fix: scl_low = t_low + t_f (count the fall time once) — OpenTitan PR #21765.
artifact.impact: SCL held low one t_f too long every cycle; bus slower than configured, timing budget violated.
